phrases: As-salāmu ʿalaykum — Peace be upon you (formal greeting)
Dress modestly in public spaces, covering shoulders and knees, especially when visiting mosques or traditional areas
Remove shoes when entering mosques and some traditional homes
Use your right hand for greetings, eating, and passing items, as the left hand is considered unclean
Avoid public displays of affection, as Qatar is a conservative Muslim country
During Ramadan, refrain from eating, drinking, or smoking in public during daylight hours out of respect
safety_tips: INDIAN EMBASSY DETAILS: Embassy of India, Doha - Address: Al Muntazah Area, Street No. 869, Building No. 17, Zone 56, Doha, Qatar. Phone: +974-4467-3502/3503. Emergency Helpline: +974-3313-6185. Email: cons.doha@mea.gov.in. Working Hours: Sunday-Thursday 8:00 AM - 4:00 PM

EMERGENCY CONTACTS: Police Emergency: 999. Ambulance/Medical Emergency: 999. Fire Department: 999. Tourist Police: +974-4439-8888. Hamad Medical Corporation Emergency: +974-4439-4444. Qatar Red Crescent: +974-4442-7777

COMMON SCAMS TO AVOID: Fake job offers promising high salaries - verify through official channels. Overcharging by taxi drivers - use Karwa taxis or ride-hailing apps. Fake gold/jewelry sellers in souks - buy only from licensed dealers. Credit card skimming at ATMs - use bank ATMs inside buildings. Fake police asking for documents - real police have proper ID

DOCUMENT SAFETY: Always carry original passport and Qatar ID (if resident). Keep photocopies separate from originals. Store digital copies in cloud storage. Register with Indian Embassy upon arrival for long stays. Ensure passport has minimum 6 months validity

TRANSPORTATION SAFETY: Use official Karwa taxis (maroon and turquoise) or licensed ride-hailing apps like Uber/Careem. Avoid unmarked vehicles. Metro and buses are safe and reliable. Keep taxi receipts for complaints if needed

CULTURAL SENSITIVITY: Dress modestly, especially in religious areas and government buildings. Avoid public displays of affection. Respect local customs during Ramadan. Photography restrictions apply to government buildings and military areas. Alcohol consumption only in licensed venues

FINANCIAL SAFETY: Use ATMs inside banks or malls. Notify your Indian bank about Qatar travel. Keep emergency cash in Qatari Riyals. Avoid street money changers - use authorized exchange centers. Credit cards widely accepted but carry cash for small vendors

HEALTH PRECAUTIONS: Drink bottled or filtered water. Carry basic medications as some Indian medicines may not be available. Health insurance mandatory - ensure coverage. Hamad Medical Corporation provides quality healthcare. Summer temperatures can exceed 45°C - stay hydrated

ACCOMMODATION SAFETY: Book through reputable platforms or agencies. Verify hotel licenses and reviews. Keep valuables in hotel safes. Check fire exits and emergency procedures. Report any suspicious activities to hotel management

COMMUNICATION: Local SIM cards available from Ooredoo and Vodafone Qatar. Free WiFi widely available in malls and hotels. Download offline maps and translation apps. Learn basic Arabic phrases for emergencies

LEGAL AWARENESS: Qatar has strict laws on drugs - zero tolerance policy. Alcohol laws are strict - only consume in licensed venues. Respect photography laws - no photos of people without permission. Blasphemy and inappropriate social media posts can lead to legal issues

WOMEN TRAVELERS: Qatar is generally safe for women travelers. Dress conservatively, covering shoulders and knees. Use reputable transportation. Stay in well-reviewed accommodations. Trust your instincts and avoid isolated areas at night

BUSINESS TRAVELERS: Carry business cards and proper documentation. Respect local business hours (Sunday-Thursday). Friday is the holy day - most businesses closed. Ramadan affects business hours significantly. Networking events often held in hotels

TOURIST AREAS SAFETY: Souq Waqif is generally safe but watch for pickpockets. The Pearl and West Bay are upscale and secure. Corniche is well-patrolled and safe for walking. Desert safari tours - use licensed operators only

EMERGENCY PREPAREDNESS: Save all emergency numbers in your phone. Know your hotel address in Arabic. Carry emergency contact information. Keep some cash for emergencies. Know location of nearest hospital and Indian Embassy
connectivity_tips: Qatar's major telecom providers are Ooredoo and Vodafone Qatar, both offering excellent 4G/5G coverage throughout Doha and most of Qatar with minimal dead zones
Purchase prepaid SIM cards at Hamad International Airport upon arrival - both Ooredoo and Vodafone have counters in the arrivals hall open 24/7
Tourist SIM cards typically cost 30-50 QAR (₹650-1100) for 7-day plans with 5-10GB data, calls, and SMS included
Bring your passport for SIM card registration - it's mandatory and the process takes 5-10 minutes at official stores
eSIM options are available from both providers if your phone supports it - can be activated instantly without visiting a store
Ooredoo generally offers better coverage in remote areas, while Vodafone Qatar often has competitive pricing for data-heavy plans
Buy SIM cards only from official stores, airport counters, or authorized retailers to avoid fake/inactive cards
Major shopping malls like City Center, Villaggio, and Landmark have telecom stores for SIM purchases and top-ups
Data speeds in Doha typically range from 50-200 Mbps on 4G/5G networks, suitable for video calls and streaming
Consider 30-day plans (70-120 QAR/₹1500-2600) if staying longer, as they offer better value than extending weekly plans
Free WiFi is widely available in malls, hotels, restaurants, and the Doha Metro, reducing reliance on mobile data
International roaming from Indian carriers can be expensive (₹500-1000/day) - local SIM cards are much more economical
Top-up vouchers are available at grocery stores, petrol stations, and exchange houses if you need to add credit
Both providers offer English customer service and have mobile apps for account management and recharging
Network coverage extends well beyond Doha to tourist areas like The Pearl, West Bay, Souq Waqif, and desert safari locations
health_tips: Required Vaccines: No specific vaccinations are mandatory for Indian travelers entering Qatar/Doha. However, ensure routine vaccines (MMR, DPT, flu, COVID-19) are up-to-date as per Indian guidelines.
Recommended Vaccines: Hepatitis A and B (especially if staying long-term), Typhoid (if eating at local establishments), and Meningococcal vaccine during Hajj/Umrah seasons if participating.

Food Safety: Doha has excellent food safety standards in hotels and restaurants. Stick to bottled water initially to avoid stomach upset from mineral content differences. Street food is generally safe but start gradually.
Water Precautions: Tap water in Doha is safe and meets international standards, but bottled water is recommended for the first few days to allow your system to adjust to different mineral content.

Climate Health Tips: Stay hydrated in the desert climate, use strong sunscreen (SPF 30+), wear light-colored loose clothing, and avoid outdoor activities during peak heat (11 AM - 4 PM) especially May-September.
Healthcare System: Qatar has world-class healthcare facilities. Carry travel insurance and keep emergency numbers handy. Hamad Medical Corporation operates the main public hospitals.
Medication Guidelines: Bring prescription medications in original packaging with doctor's letter. Some medications legal in India may require permits in Qatar - check with Qatar embassy beforehand.
Air Quality: Doha occasionally experiences dust storms. Those with respiratory conditions should carry inhalers and consider masks during dusty periods.
General Precautions: No major disease risks. Practice standard hygiene, wash hands regularly, and be aware that medical costs can be high without insurance.
